intro.value: Eq. 13 is not a new self-consistency equation, and it is not derived from Eq. 12. It is a concrete choice for the natural-frequency distribution g of omega.

The point of the Lorentzian example is that it makes the integrals above explicitly computable, so the Kuramoto model no longer gives only an onset threshold K sub c but also a closed-form synchronized branch.

left.value: Eq. 13 chooses g of omega to be a Lorentzian centered at zero. So the population has the most oscillators near zero detuning, with fewer oscillators as absolute value of omega grows.
The parameter gamma sets the spread. Larger gamma means a broader distribution of natural frequencies, so the oscillators are more heterogeneous.
Unlike a Gaussian, the Lorentzian has heavier tails, so large detunings remain comparatively more common.
Eq. 12 already says the onset threshold depends only on g of 0: K sub c equals 2 over pi times g of 0.
Eq. 13 enters only now: we substitute the Lorentzian choice for g into Eq. 12. For the Lorentzian, g of 0 is 1 over pi gamma, so the threshold becomes K sub c equals 2 gamma.
Physically, broader frequency spread means it is harder to synchronize the population, so increasing gamma increases the critical coupling.
For a general frequency distribution, Eq. 11 usually only gives implicit information about r, and near K sub c one often settles for the square-root scaling law in Eq. 14.
For the Lorentzian, Kuramoto could evaluate the integrals exactly. That produces a closed-form branch for the synchronized state.

So Eq. 13 matters because it turns the general threshold story into an explicit solved example: below K sub c there is incoherence, and above K sub c the order parameter follows a simple square-root law.
